title: The Nominative Case (Nominativ)
description: What is a 'Case'? Start here by learning how German marks the 'Doer' (the Subject) of a sentence.
explanation:
**HOOK**
In English, you say 'I am' instead of 'Me am'. German works exactly the same way but goes a little bit further—it uses 'nametags' called Cases!

**CONCEPT**
The Nominativ case is just your way of showing who the DOER is. It points out the main star of the sentence—the person or thing taking action.

**RULE**
This is the base form, so you just use the standard dictionary words you already know! Absolutely nothing changes.

tables.title: Nominativ Default Articles
tables.note: Look closely: They are perfectly untouched. Treat 'Nominativ' as your home base.

deep_notes.title: WATCH OUT
deep_notes.text:
Most beginners forget that the verb 'sein' (to be) acts like an equal sign (=). Because it links two things together (like 'He is a man'), BOTH sides stay in the Nominativ case!
This one trips up almost everyone at first — and that is completely normal.

exercises.question: Welche Aufgabe hat der 'Nominativ'?
exercises.hint: Who is the star or DOER of the sentence?
exercises.explanation: The Nominativ case is simply the base form used for the star of the sentence—the Subject!

exercises.hint: The dog is doing the action. Hund is masculine.
exercises.explanation: The dog is doing the sleeping, so it stays in the base Nominativ (der).

exercises.question: Achtung! Warum steht 'Tisch' im Satz 'Das ist DER TISCH' im Nominativ?
options: Weil es ein indirektes Objekt ist
Es ist falsch, es muss 'den Tisch' heißen
exercises.hint: Was stand in der Theorie über 'ist/sein'?
exercises.explanation: Verbs like 'sein' connect two subjects. So 'der Tisch' stays Nominativ!

exercises.question: Ordne die bestimmten und unbestimmten Artikel zu:

exercises.hint: Since 'ist' connects both sides, 'das' just uses its normal Nominativ word 'ein'.
exercises.explanation: Again, the verb 'ist' means both sides are Nominativ! So 'das' stays 'ein'.

exercises.question: In welchem Satz gibt es ZWEI Nominative (Gleichheitszeichen)?
exercises.hint: Look for the verb 'ist' (to be) which links two things.
exercises.explanation: Because of 'ist', both 'Der Mann' and 'Arzt' (doctor) are Nominativ!

exercises.question: Ordne den Satz:
exercises.hint: Start with the subject action-doer (Ein Mann).
This is plural! Plural always takes the same base article.
exercises.explanation: Plural base Nominative article is ALWAYS 'die'!

exercises.question: Es ist nass und wir trinken es jeden Tag. ____ ____ ist sehr kalt.
